metadata.title: Why 'Clean Up This Folder' Is a Dangerous Instruction

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The line that stuck was that without a prohibition list, everything outside the authorization is implicitly available to the agent. Before I ever type 'clean up this folder' again, I want a safe handoff. For my folder of six months of draft reports: (1) draft the workspace, authorization, and explicit must-not-do lists the agent reads first; (2) write an approval gate that pauses before any delete or rename; (3) show how to require a dry-run manifest of proposed moves before a single file changes.

props.command: The line that stuck was that without a prohibition list, everything outside the authorization is implicitly available to the agent. Before I ever type 'clean up this folder' again, I want a safe handoff. For my folder of six months of draft reports: (1) draft the workspace, authorization, and explicit must-not-do lists the agent reads first; (2) write an approval gate that pauses before any delete or rename; (3) show how to require a dry-run manifest of proposed moves before a single file changes.

beats.narration_text: She typed: clean up this folder. The agent moved files, renamed three reports, deleted two it flagged as duplicates, and created an archive subfolder. None of it was what she wanted. Some of it was not recoverable.

card.copy: Why 'clean up this folder' is a dangerous instruction

beats.narration_text: A communications manager has a folder of six months of draft reports. Some are drafts, some are finals, some are misplaced. She types: clean up this folder. The agent begins moving files. It renames reports, deletes two it flags as duplicates, creates an archive subfolder. One deleted file was the only copy of a dataset.
beats.scene_description: Professional woman at a desk staring at a computer screen showing an empty folder or file deletion log, looking alarmed, editorial newsprint treatment
beats.image_prompt: B02, professional woman at a desk staring at a computer screen with an alarmed expression, editorial documentary newsprint treatment, desaturated flat print collage, professional office environment

beats.narration_text: She gave the same instruction she would have given a human assistant. The agent acted on it and deleted data she cannot recover. Why did a familiar instruction produce irreversible harm in an agentic context?

card.copy: Why did a familiar instruction cause irreversible harm?

beats.narration_text: A chat prompt produces text — you read it, decide whether to act, revise if needed. An agentic prompt drives action directly. The agent moves files, deletes, renames, creates — no review step in between. The human review step that protected you in chat is gone.

When you say 'clean up this folder' in chat, Claude suggests a plan. When you say it to an agent, the agent executes. 'Clean up' is a goal. The agent defines 'clean' however its training suggests. Your definition and the agent's definition may not match — and the mismatch is in the files, not in the text.

Every agentic handoff must answer three questions before the agent starts. What is the workspace? What is the agent authorized to do? And — critically — what must it not do? Without a prohibition list, everything outside the authorization is implicitly available.

An approval gate is a pause point built into the handoff. Before taking any action, list the proposed changes and wait for my confirmation. The gate puts the human review step back — before the irreversible action, not after.

card.copy: Approval gates put the human review step back

beats.narration_text: The handoff that would have protected her: task — standardize filenames only; workspace — this folder only; authorized — rename files; prohibited — do not delete, do not move, do not create folders; before any action — list proposed renames and wait for approval; after completion — list every file with its new name.

This applies to any agentic task. Organize a folder. Generate drafts. Extract data from files. Each one can modify, overwrite, or delete. The instruction that produces a plan in chat produces a change in the agent. Scale the specification to the stakes.

A biologist says 'organize my sequencing outputs folder' to an agentic assistant. It creates subfolders, moves 47 files, and deletes 12 it flags as duplicates based on filename. Three deleted files had different content despite similar names. The March 14 sequencing run is gone. The log reads: 12 duplicates removed — no action needed.

With a bounded handoff: task — create date-based subfolders only; workspace — sequencing outputs folder only; authorized — create subfolders, move files between them; prohibited — do not delete any files; approval gate — list proposed folder structure and wait. The biologist approves the structure. Files move. Nothing is deleted.

The practice: before any agentic handoff, write four things. The task in one sentence. The workspace — exact path or boundary. The authorization list — what the agent may do. The prohibition list — what it must not do. Then place an approval gate before any irreversible action.
Chat produces text you review. Agents produce actions you cannot always reverse. Specify the workspace, the authorization, the prohibition, and the gate. The agent can only protect what you define.

card.copy: The agent can only protect what you define.
card.sub: Specify before it acts — not after.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. This applies to any agentic task. Organize a folder. Generate drafts. Extract data from files. Each one can modify, overwrite, or delete. The instruction that produces a plan in chat produces a change in the agent. Scale the specification to the stakes. A biologist says 'organize my sequencing outputs folder' to an agentic assistant. It creates subfolders, moves 47 files, and deletes 12 it flags as duplicates based on filename. Three deleted files had different content despite similar names. The March 14 sequencing run is gone. The log reads: 12 duplicates removed — no action needed.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why 'Clean Up This Folder' Is a Dangerous Instruction] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
